topic: Making Slot Attention Work with Single-View Features
problem: Slot Attention requires differentiated input tokens so softmax competition can assign different features to different slots. When all input tokens are identical copies of a shared feature vector (e.g. one audio embedding repeated), slots collapse to the same output and no specialization occurs.
solution: Apply per-slot learnable projections: each of N slots gets its own independent Linear layer that projects the shared feature vector into a slightly different view. This creates the competitive gradient needed for slot specialization.
code_pattern: self.per_slot_input_proj = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(D, D) for _ in range(N)]); outputs = torch.stack([proj[i](x) for i in range(N)], dim=2)
pitfalls: NEVER use .expand(B, T, N, D) to create identical copies — this gives zero competition gradient
NEVER use a single shared Linear for all slots — same gradient, no differentiation

topic: Inserting New Layers into Pre-Trained Models Without Gradient Explosion
problem: When inserting randomly initialized layers into an already-trained model, the output distribution differs drastically from what downstream layers expect. This causes large prediction errors and gradient spikes (norm 50-220x). Lowering learning rate does NOT fix the root cause — the distribution mismatch persists.
root_cause: Random init (e.g. Kaiming uniform) produces outputs with variance ~10x the input range. The downstream layers were trained on inputs with variance ~1x. This is a distribution shift at inference time, not just a learning rate problem.
solution: Initialize new layers to be near-identity: W = I + noise_small, b = 0. Output ≈ Input so downstream layers see a familiar distribution. The small noise provides the initial asymmetry needed for gradient flow.
code_pattern:
for lin in new_layers:
    nn.init.normal_(lin.weight, std=0.001)
    lin.weight.data.add_(torch.eye(D))
    nn.init.zeros_(lin.bias)
why_it_works: Output ≈ Input + N(0, 0.001) → downstream layers see familiar distribution
Small noise gives non-zero gradients for learning
Different per-slot noise seeds = initial differentiation for competition
anti_patterns: Do NOT wrap near-identity in LayerNorm — LN normalizes away the identity property
Do NOT add GELU/ReLU after near-identity — nonlinearity destroys the distribution match

topic: Using Different Learning Rates for Different Parameter Groups in PyTorch
problem: When adding new parameter groups (e.g. per-slot projections) to an existing model, those new layers need a different learning rate than the pretrained body. Setting the same LR for all causes either the new layers to destabilize or the pretrained layers to learn too slowly.
solution: Use PyTorch's param_groups with separate LRs. New surgical layers should have 3-5x lower LR than the pretrained body.
code_pattern:
optimizer = Adam([
    {'params': pretrained_params, 'lr': base_lr},
    {'params': new_layer_params, 'lr': base_lr / 5},
])

topic: Correctly Resuming CosineAnnealingLR After LR Change at Checkpoint Load
problem: When changing the learning rate after loading a checkpoint, `CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, ..., last_epoch=saved_step)` internally captures `base_lrs` from the optimizer BEFORE the LR override is applied, causing the scheduler to continue from the old LR value.
root_cause: CosineAnnealingLR computes its internal schedule from `self.base_lrs` captured at construction time. The `last_epoch` parameter only sets the step position, not the amplitude.
solution: Do NOT use the `last_epoch` constructor parameter. Instead, override the optimizer LRs first, then create the scheduler and set last_epoch after construction.
code_pattern:
# 1. Override optimizer LRs
for pg in optimizer.param_groups: pg['lr'] = target_lr
# 2. Create fresh scheduler (captures correct base_lrs)
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=total_steps, eta_min=1e-6)
# 3. Jump to correct step
scheduler.last_epoch = saved_global_step
why_it_works: The constructor captures the correct (already-overridden) base_lrs. Setting last_epoch after construction positions the scheduler at the right step without re-capturing old base_lrs.
